channel.title: +GA on आगमे ब्राह्मे विश्वासटिप्पन्यः
channel.description: Recent content in +GA on आगमे ब्राह्मे विश्वासटिप्पन्यः

item.title: Appayya accommodation
item.description:
Source: TW

Will only mention a few points:

Śaiva term

When Śaiva is used distinctly but together with pāśupata, Kāpālika, kālāmukha, kāruka, etc, it refers to the Siddhānta. This is a conventional usage.

Vaidika-Pāśupatam

Appayya expressly refers—in his Śivatattvaviveka—to Vaidika-Pāśupatam as the acceptable path since it (allegedly) upholds that Śiva is both the material and instrumental cause of the jagat. This would exclude the Saiddhāntika canon.

Aśrauta

The reference to an “Aśrauta” Śaiva canon arises in the Vāyavīya (final book of the Śivapurāṇa) but the reference is not at all negative. It is called Aśrauta because it is not tethered to the Veda and has its own independent divine status. This Aśrauta canon is defined in that chapter of the Vāyavīya as Kāmika, etc—again, the Saiddhāntika canon.

item.title: maTha-affiliation
item.description:
Source: TW

Source: TW

In other words, are Smartas and Advaitins necessarily one and the same?

No, that is not the case. The Advaita-Maṭha-affiliation of Smārta Gṛhasthas is of recent provenance (500 years ago or less).

Smārta stalwart Rāmasubba Śāstri was a Smārta but defended Vaiṣṇavam. There were & still are other Smārtas who defended & defend Śaivam. Some Smārtas took up Śivadīkṣā and became Mahaśaivas too.

Term origin

What would have differentiated a “Smārta” from “others” is a question dependent on the era as the definitions of both have evolved considerably. Kumārila’s time (when Pūrva-Mīmāmsakas were still strong as a group); an era when most Vipras accepted Veda’s authority at some level. The question then turns to secondary texts:

item.title: SmArta-roga
item.description:
AparArka seemed to had some unnecessary hatred toward shaiviam. But not surprising as he was poster boy for smartarogi:

The accursed Aparāditya-1’s own descendants, Mallikārjuna and Aparāditya-2 realized the worthlessness of his inane proclamations and had Saiddhāntika Śaivācāryas as Rājagurus.

The Roga suffered by the likes of Aparāditya-1 was not some isolated instance. Rather, it was part of a larger phenomenon that went on for hundreds of years and did untold damage to the Hindu fabric. Some correction did arrive in the 1600s but it was a case of “too little, too late”.
